participants.extracted: This proof-of-concept randomized trial included 20 preterm infants born at <30 weeks’ gestation and <1000 g birth weight who were receiving supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula without mechanical ventilation. Infants were eligible if they had episodes of desaturation or hypoxaemia and were excluded if they were on mechanical ventilation, had congenital anomalies, or if parental consent was not obtained. Ten infants were randomized to the Auto-Mixer® group and 10 to manual blender routine care. The groups were similar at baseline, with mean gestational ages of 27.3 ± 1.7 vs 27.7 ± 1.7 weeks and birth weights of 785 ± 47.7 vs 763 ± 45.8 g, and median postnatal ages of 9 vs 8 days, respectively.

interventions.extracted: Auto-Mixer® automated oxygen titration was evaluated in preterm infants <30 weeks’ gestation and <1000 g who were receiving supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula without mechanical ventilation. In the intervention group, the device continuously monitored SpO2 using signal-extraction pulse oximetry and automatically adjusted FiO2 to maintain oxygen saturation within the clinician-set target range of 85% to 93%, with response timing set at 10 seconds and FiO2 changes set at 1% to 5%. The system also reduced FiO2 by 2% when SpO2 stayed at or above the mean target for at least 3 minutes, and it triggered alarms if saturation remained outside limits for 40 seconds. The comparison group received usual care with manual FiO2 adjustment by bedside healthcare staff according to routine NICU practice over the same 12-hour monitoring period.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure oxygenation control outcomes during the 12-hour study period in preterm infants receiving supplemental oxygen without mechanical ventilation. The primary outcome was the percentage of time that SpO2 was within the prespecified target range of 85% to 93% over the 12 h recording period. Secondary outcomes were the number of nursing/manual interventions required to adjust oxygen, the amount of oxygen delivered to the infant during the 12 h period (FiO2), and variability/dispersion of SpO2 over the 12 h recording period.
